text.content:
Scene 1 · The Morning Review

The Refectory of the Little Palace, the second bell after dawn. The morning after the crane-and-coins letter.

The Refectory at the second bell after dawn. The evidence has been laid out for daylight work.

Overnight, Caelith brought Haskur's possessions up out of the cellar Strong Room and laid them along the great oak table — not as treasure, but as evidence, each object on its own square of numbered linen. When the party comes down to breakfast, this is what they find, and a stranger already sitting with the morning's tea.

Morning comes gray over Loftwick. The Little Palace smells of beeswax, hearth smoke, boiled coffee, and sealed wax. Outside, the city has already begun celebrating a victory it does not understand — bells somewhere beyond the garden wall, hooves over street stones, a clerk in the hall arguing softly with a militia sergeant about who is allowed in.

In the Refectory the brass chandeliers are low-lit, the lacquered paneling drinking what little light comes through the leaded windows. Caelith Dunivar has laid the dead man's possessions across the long table. Each object rests on a square of linen. Each square is numbered in his precise hand. The room feels less like a sitting chamber now and more like a battlefield after all the blood has learned to write.

A woman is already at the table, a cup of pale tea balanced on one knee. Her dress is the color of a forest at dusk; a small silver leaf rests at her collar. She does not stand when you enter. She inclines her head a careful inch and sets the cup down.

Caelith looks up. "I have good news," he says. "The man you fought was almost certainly Haskur Vandrell." He taps one gloved finger beside a ruined length of dark wood. "The bad news is that Haskur Vandrell has been dead before."

The woman speaks before he can go on, in a voice you have to lean toward. "Good morning. I am Trina. I advise Caelith on people. I am sorry about the envelope at breakfast. I expect we will get to it last."

GM: Let the party settle and take the room in. Trina's introduction is deliberately light; she will not steer the morning unless asked. If the party's warlock, Alicia, is among them, Trina quietly offers her a cup of tea and nothing else — the first thread of a relationship that runs through the whole chapter (see The Mentor).

GM — location: the morning review happens in the Refectory (M4) — long polished-oak table, brass chandeliers, lacquered paneling, leaded windows. The grandeur is deliberate; Caelith likes the room because it makes the evidence look like what it is — accounting that has cost lives. The evidence stays here for the rest of the morning. The cellar Strong Room (C5) returns to being storage; Caelith carries items back down at the end of each working session.

Transitions to: Caelith's Hand-of-the-Duke speech (next page), then the daylight examination of the seven items.

Scene 1 · Caelith's Hand-of-the-Duke Speech

Once the party has found chairs, Caelith says his piece. Read it in his voice — measured, every sentence weighed as though it's going on a record — and don't rush the four turns of it. He sets a small flat brass token on the table as he begins: a quill etched above a closed gate.

The Hand of the Duke. The Yeomanry maintains no formal title for it.

"Last night I told you what was true and not for public ears. This morning I will tell you what is true and not yet, by any formal record, anyone's. I prefer that we agree on a standard of trust before we keep working.

"I am, by quiet appointment of the Duke, the Hand. The Yeomanry maintains no formal title for it — you will not find it in any document our Council reads. My office at the Audit Hall is the body of the work. The Hand is the part that goes outside our borders. You have been corresponding with that part since you walked through the Lesser Gate.

"Haskur Vandrell is not a man I learned about last week. He has been on the back of my desk for nine years, and in that time he has died twice — pulled back, like he was pulled back from you, by a contingency someone else laid down ahead of him. Once at sea off Onnwal, in 575; he went into the water between deck and boat and we wrote him drowned. Once on a Hardby tavern floor in 580, bleeding out, until a barmaid I had never identified set a hand on his chest and he was simply not there. When I went to find the barmaid, she was also not there.

"To underwrite a man twice in nine years and keep him moving between cities and pretexts costs more than a guild has, and more than a noble cousin has. It costs the kind of money you only see when an institution is being used like a wallet. That is why your fight matters. He is not the man who made him. He is the man someone keeps making.

"Last night you saw a letter of credit drawn six weeks before your fight, in Rel Astra, endorsed to him or his agent. This morning, in better light, we have seen a great deal more. I would like to walk you through it — and I would like you to tell me what you see that I have not. If, after that, you still wish to take the eastern commission, I will show you three ways to travel and the cost of each. If you wish to leave us to it, I will pay you in full and you will hear nothing further from me. I say it again, in front of Trina, so that no one mishears."

The four beats of the speech

  • Beat 1 — the title. Caelith names his real role: the Hand of the Duke, the part of the work that goes outside the Yeomanry's borders.
  • Beat 2 — the pattern. Haskur has died twice before; the conspiracy is at least nine years old; each pull-back was paid for in advance.
  • Beat 3 — the scale. The money behind those pull-backs is institution-scale, not personal. Someone is using an institution like a wallet.
  • Beat 4 — the choice, restated, with a witness. Take the eastern commission, or be paid in full and hear no more. Trina is named as witness.

GM notes

  • GM: Caelith leaves the brass token on the table for the rest of the morning and pockets it only when the party leaves the room. A character watching closely (passive Perception 14) understands he is showing it to them, not flashing it.
  • GM: He will answer factual questions and refuse speculation. Two questions usually come up — keep his answers exactly this firm.
  • GM: Trina says nothing during the speech. She is the witness. If Caelith glances at her at the end, she nods once.
  • GM: He is right to worry about a leak inside his own office, and the chapter will keep brushing against it — but he never names a name, and neither do you.

If the party asks the obvious follow-ups

"Why us?" — "Because you have already done the part of this work that requires being a stranger to the Yeomanry, and the part I cannot trust to my own clerks is the part where the strangers do the writing."

"Who do you suspect in your own office?" — "I will not name a clerk to you whom I cannot prove. If I named one, and you treated them as proven, I would have made you the wrong kind of Hand."

"What is Trina to this?" — "Trina is a private citizen who has spent twenty years funding poets and watching how people enter rooms. She is here because I asked her to come. She will say what she sees when she sees it. She will not say more than that."

What Caelith says plainly during the morning

As the party works through the items, Caelith will say each of these things, unprompted, when the topic comes near. They are the framing he wants the table to land on:

  • "Haskur Vandrell is not a noble, not a priest, not a general, and not a court agent in any ordinary sense."
  • "He is what powerful people use when they need something done, but not known."
  • "A courier. A fixer. A negotiator of debts no honest court would recognize."
  • "He appears in records near failed prosecutions, missing witnesses, spoiled harvest reports, and mercantile disputes that resolved too cleanly."

Scene 1 · The Seven Items

Now the heart of the morning: the party reads the evidence by daylight. Let them pick objects up and pass them around. Each one rewards a look, and most reward a closer look; weave the checks below into the conversation rather than calling for a row of rolls. Caelith offers a single dry line over each as the party gets to it — those lines are the framing you want them to carry out of the room.

1 · The burned spell focus

A palm-sized length of dark wood carved with concentric grooves, intact but used hard: one face carries a long scorch down the inside of a groove, with a bead of melted beeswax fused into the char.

  • Arcana DC 13: A focus only scorches like this when a contingency-class spell has been channeled through it — used as the conduit of a pre-laid escape, not as an ordinary casting tool.
  • Arcana DC 16: A sigil pressed into the wax-bead matches nothing in Haskur's own notes: a stylized three-rayed sun closing into a fist. Whoever laid the contingency, it was not him.
  • GM: The sun-into-fist sigil is the mark of the conspirators' patron school of magic. Do not name it. A character who pushes for an identification gets only: "a school I have seen on two unrelated documents in nine years, and never once with a name attached." That is the whole reveal here. It is a Phase 3-and-later thread.

Caelith, quietly: "This was not instinct. This was a contract being honored."

GM only — the wax-bead sigil pressed into the focus's char. A school no Yeomanry sage can name.

2 · The false letter of passage

From Haskur's wallet of correspondence, a Yeomanry letter of safe passage naming the bearer Haskur Voren, Agricultural Assessor, in service to the Loftwick Bureau of Crop Estimation — real vellum, real wax, a chop that matches.

  • Investigation DC 12: The vellum is Yeomanry, the seal real wax, the chop matches.
  • Investigation DC 15: The signature, checked against the Audit Hall's files, belongs to the previous Bureau Chief, who retired in 581 — and the letter is dated last summer.
  • History DC 14: The Bureau never sends assessors out on itinerant routes; they sit and audit by mail. A man carrying this through inns and toll-gates would never be questioned by a watchman — and never dare show his face at a real Bureau audit. Whoever forged it knew exactly that gap.
  • GM: Real Yeomanry vellum, real wax, the retired Chief's signature on file, and the procedural knowledge that this cover would never bring scrutiny. That is an inside job.

Player handout — the forged letter as recovered.

Caelith, dry as winter: "Agricultural assessor. Very funny, or very grave. We will know which when we learn who pays for the harvest he was counting."

3 · The clipped coin

A heavy silver coin, not Yeomanry mint, its edges shaved thin — and on the clipped edge, a tiny countermark punched into the metal: a tower above three waves.

  • Investigation DC 12: A merchant's eye reads the countermark as a private exchange mark — someone has "weighed this coin in" against an internal account.
  • History DC 15: A scholar of the eastern ports places it on the Rel Astran coast.
  • History DC 17: A very good one names the house outright: Cindren & Vhal, Bonded Factors — the same firm whose crane-and-coins seal countersigned last night's letter of credit. This isn't money. It's a token of account.

The countermark — tower above three waves. Cindren & Vhal's internal clearing-mark.

Caelith, holding it to the light: "This is not a coin. It is a receipt pretending to be money."

4 · The black-lacquered tube

Nested inside Haskur's brass map-case is a second, smaller cylinder — a finger-length tube of black lacquer, sealed at one end with unbroken red wax. It's a courier's vessel: shockproof, watertight, made to ride hidden inside a larger tube.

  • Investigation DC 10: Courier-grade vessel — shockproof, watertight, designed to ride hidden inside another tube as plausible deniability.
  • Arcana DC 13: The seal carries a minor abjuration: break it without the passphrase and it discharges a single command — the word is "Forget" — at whoever opened it (Wisdom save DC 13, single use).
  • Arcana or History DC 17: The craft itself, the seal-glyph and the lacquer laid down in seven thin coats where convention asks for four, reads to any sage as elven work — but in a tradition no one present can place. The most accurate sentence anyone can offer is, "Elven. But peculiar."
  • Inside, once opened: a strip of vellum and a fragment of ledger paper (items 5 and 6).
  • Investigation DC 14: The interior has been deliberately wiped clean — but a faint heat-darkened smudge remains on the inner lacquer.
  • Arcana DC 15: The smudge is consistent with heat-reactive ink — a message meant to be warmed, read once, and lost. Whatever was in there has been read, removed, and burned. The tube wasn't just a container; it was a relay.
  • GM: Whichever PC opens the tube triggers the command. If they fail the save, they spend their next turn following the suggestion — likely losing a round of evidence handling, possibly setting down what they were holding. It is not lethal; it is administrative. This is the mood of the conspiracy.
  • GM (campaign-level): The tube and its seal are not elven. They are Drow work. In this world Drow are an ancient legend no one alive remembers, so the best identification anyone can make is "elven, but peculiar." Do not name "Drow" in Phase 2 under any roll. If a PC casts legend lore or similar, the response is a long fragmentary verse about a people who walked downward and were forgotten, which the caster cannot place. The peculiarity is the entire reveal in this phase.

Caelith, snapping it shut between two fingers: "The container was bait. Whatever was inside before the seal was last set, we will not know without the man who set it."

5 · The vellum strip — "HARBOR BEFORE THRONE"

Inside the tube, a finger-width strip of fine vellum. Four words in clear capitals: HARBOR BEFORE THRONE. No date, no signature.

  • History DC 13: The phrasing is older than current idiom; it reads as a clause from a charter or a pre-Aerdi succession formula.
  • Religion DC 15: The phrase appears in two surviving fragments of the Aerdi Concordat of the Bay, a fourth-century imperial settlement law holding that a port-city's harbor council must be seated before any newly-named throne can collect its revenues — the harbor outranks the crown until the crown acknowledges the harbor.
  • Investigation DC 14: Read as code rather than law, it is an instruction about timing: seat the harbor before any new throne can claim its dues.
  • GM: The line is a signal of intent. The conspirators are timing something against an unnamed succession. Do not resolve what throne or which harbor. The party should leave the room with the phrase memorized and uneasy. It pays off in Rel Astra, two chapters from now.

Player handout — the vellum strip as found inside the lacquered tube.

Caelith, half-smiling without amusement: "Power flows through ports before crowns notice. That is older than the Yeomanry, older than Rel Astra. It is also a slogan — which is what people write down when they intend to do something the law will not yet name."

6 · The ledger fragment

Also from inside the tube: a torn corner of double-entry paper, no header, no date, three lines in three different hands —

VANDRELL settled
MORAVEN delayed
GILST transferred

  • Investigation DC 13: Three clerks wrote those lines; this is not one person's notes but a status board, kept somewhere a small group passes through.
  • Investigation DC 14: "Settled," "delayed," and "transferred" are accounting verbs. Cross-referenced against last night's letter of credit, VANDRELL settled matches the very date Haskur's escrow was drawn down — whoever wrote it had live access to the firm's settlement records.
  • Investigation DC 14, on the paper itself: There is not a single sum anywhere on the fragment. A real ledger carries figures on every line. This one doesn't, because the money was never the point.
  • GM: Three names — Vandrell, Moraven, Gilst — drive the rest of the chapter. Make sure the table leaves the morning holding them.

Player handout — the ledger fragment as found.

Caelith, after a long silence: "There are no sums. That means the money was not the point. People were. They were tracking who was where — and who was no longer."

7 · The hidden seed pouch

Last, Caelith cuts open the lining of Haskur's spellbook in front of the party — he found a small cloth pouch sewn into the back cover overnight. Inside: a handful of wheat seeds and a folded scrap reading "Seed for the dry barony."

  • Nature DC 13: The grain is durum, but blighted sterile at the husk — display seeds, not viable stock.
  • Nature DC 16: Sterile durum is exactly what's used to evidence a harvest that does not exist to an inspector.
  • Investigation DC 14: "The dry barony" is no Yeomanry estate — it's an old colloquial name for a rainfall-poor, grain-importing region of the South Province of the Great Kingdom: a perfect customer for receipts on grain nobody grew.
  • Nature DC 17 (or with herbalist's tools): The husks have been chemically washed to fool a quick eye and fail a slow one. The treatment is itself a forgery.
  • GM: The pouch ties Haskur to a false-crop scheme. The verb "delayed" on Tamsin Moraven's line is now ominous: a grain factor who could expose a false-crop scheme has been "delayed" in the firm's accounting.

Caelith, turning the treated husks in his fingers: "Either disguise — or truth wearing a mask. I cannot decide which is more frightening."

Where this leads

The party has spent a morning turning objects into questions, and the ledger fragment has handed them the answer to where to look next. Push gently off the table and toward the wall:

Caelith sets the torn ledger-corner back on its square of linen and looks at the three names a moment longer. "Three names, three hands, no sums," he says. "That is not a banker's ledger. It is a roster. And I keep the rolls that turn names into men — who they were, where they posted, where they died. They are downstairs, behind the library wall." He stands, and takes up a candle. "Come. Let us go and make these three into people."

Lead straight into Scene 2. Trina comes with them.

pages.name: Scene 2 · Behind the Library Wall (Records Alcove)

text.content:
Scene 2 · Behind the Library Wall

The Records Alcove, off the Little Palace library. Late morning.

The Records Alcove behind the Library's panel. Trina's finger is on the page that turns three names into a firm.

This is the pivot of the whole chapter, and it does one job supremely well: it turns three bare names into two streets the party can walk to. Run it unhurried; this is the moment the investigation stops being Caelith's and becomes theirs.

Behind the writing desk at the library window, a paneled section of the rear wall swings on a quiet hinge — Caelith works it without comment — and opens on a small warm room you did not see last night. A single oil lamp. Two walls of low shelving, packed with bound civic records the public library does not carry: the Yeomanry Bonded Firms Charter Roll; the Loftwick Treasury Bonds Clerk Register; Aerdi Commercial Marks & Counter-Marks, Rel Astran edition; the Grain Factor Annual Returns. Trina takes the chair at the little reading table, draws the first volume someone hands her into her lap, and licks a finger to turn a page. "Names," she says, mildly. "Let us see whose."

Let the party drive the search; Trina assists anyone who asks (her steady hand grants advantage where it helps). Three readings resolve, and each one comes with an address attached — that's the part that matters.

The firm

The crane-and-coins seal and the tower-over-waves countermark both resolve, in the marks volume, to Cindren & Vhal, Bonded Factors.

  • Investigation DC 12: The crane-and-coins seal belongs to Cindren & Vhal.
  • Investigation DC 15: The tower-above-three-waves countermark is the same house — their internal clearing-mark, used between their own offices for tokens already weighed in.
  • History DC 14 (Charter Roll): Senior partners and the real vault are in Rel Astra; a working branch sits in Hardby; and here in Loftwick only a single notary's office — a satellite, not a branch, unable to hold escrow, staffed by one bonded clerk named Hesren Vesh. The address is Wick Lane.

The dead clerk

Gilst resolves in the Bonds Clerk Register to Merro Gilst.

  • Investigation DC 11: Merro Gilst, junior Treasury bonds clerk, fifty-one years old, fifteen years in post — his last entry stamped three days before the party rode into Loftwick, and marked posting closed.
  • Investigation DC 14: In this register that phrase means death, retirement, or transfer; there is no retirement and no transfer order on file, so it means he died. Yet the certifying physician, one Reln Pollow, is not a Yeomanry coroner but a contract physician with rooms on Wick Lane.
  • Persuasion DC 13 with Caelith: He goes still at that, and admits why — he wasn't informed of the death through his own channels, and that frightens him more than he'll show. He asks the party not to call on Pollow until he's had a chance to pull the man's licensure.

The missing woman

Moraven resolves in the Grain Factor Returns to Tamsin Moraven.

  • Investigation DC 12: Tamsin Moraven, senior grain factor, twelve years in trade, her counting house on Tannerway by the eastern caravan yard — last return filed on time four months ago, nothing since.
  • Investigation DC 15 (cross-referenced with the watch register): A missing-person report against her name, eleven days old, taken by a Sergeant Halir Anwic and marked open, unescalated: she left her counting house with a bag and a cloak, told her staff she was riding to a customer's farm, never named the farm, never came back.
  • History DC 13: Her own filings are full of pointed queries about South Province grain shipments — exactly the dry-barony pattern the seed pouch points at. She was the Yeomanry's most persistent questioner of false-crop receipts, and now she's gone.

If Trina is asked about the names

She answers plainly and briefly, and one of them costs her something:

Of Cindren & Vhal: "Twice they came to me for ‘small loans,’ and twice I said no, and the second time the man who asked stood a little too long in my doorway after he had been thanked. That is not how a clean firm leaves a house."

Of Gilst: a single shake of the head. "I did not know him. I am sorry."

Of Tamsin Moraven — she pales, but her voice stays level: "Tamsin paid half her daughter's tuition out of her own purse. I paid the other half. Tamsin does not vanish on her own initiative. Find her. Please."

Where this leads

By the time the lamp's burned down, the party has two neighborhoods and six reasons to walk to them. Make the handoff theirs, not Caelith's — he stays behind on purpose:

Caelith closes the marks volume and squares it on the shelf. "Wick Lane gives you three doors worth knocking — the firm's office, the dead man's rooms, the physician who signed him away. Tannerway gives you three more — the woman's counting house, her husband's house, the sergeant who took her file and let it cool." He pauses at the panel. "I will not come. My face is known on Wick Lane; yours is not, yet. Go as the strangers you are, and come back and tell me what the city would not say to me."

GM — if the dice are unkind: don't let the addresses hang on a failed roll. Trina supplies any name the search won't give up — she knew Moraven, she remembers the firm's "odor" — and Caelith's own reading of the registers backstops the rest. No one leaves this room without both streets. From here the party chooses where to start; that choice is the chapter's first real motion, so let them make it. Most parties feel the pull of the dead clerk first — Wick Lane is where the chapter comes alive, and it's where someone is already watching the street.

What the party should leave the alcove with

  • The name Cindren & Vhal, Bonded Factors, with offices in Rel Astra (main), Hardby, and a Loftwick satellite at Wick Lane.
  • The fact that Merro Gilst is dead under suspicious paperwork, certified by a non-coronial physician on Wick Lane.
  • The fact that Tamsin Moraven has been missing for eleven days, with an open but unescalated watch file on Tannerway.
  • The fact that Moraven was actively investigating false-crop receipts on the same grain trade Haskur's seed pouch points at.

None of this proves Cindren & Vhal of conspiracy. It does establish that the firm sits at the center of three converging threads, and that the threads are actively closing — Gilst dead, Moraven missing, the Vandrell escrow already drawn down.
